splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on when actuarial assumptions must be finalized for calculating employer withdrawal liability from multiemployer pension plans. The timing directly affects how much withdrawing employers must pay, implicating billions of dollars in potential obligations across thousands of employers.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions used to calculate withdrawal liability must be selected and finalized by the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects revocation proceedings for thousands of individuals who abscond each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, allowing courts to find violations and revoke supervision even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release during absconding, and the term expires as originally set regardless of whether the supervisee fled.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face the additional gatekeeping requirement of § 2244(b)(1) — which categorically bars claims previously presented — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255. If it applies, federal prisoners are barred from re-raising any previously presented claim; if not, they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims in § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive motions under § 2255, imposing a stricter gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions, meaning federal prisoners need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s own requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The resolution affects whether such disputes can be adjudicated before elections or must wait until after, with significant implications for election administration nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates suffer a cognizable competitive injury from allegedly unlawful election rules and have standing to challenge them before an election takes place.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not suffer a sufficiently concrete and particularized injury from state election procedures to establish Article III standing for pre-election challenges.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations in criminal sentencing contexts.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps Auer/Kisor deference to agency interpretations of ambiguous criminal provisions. At least three circuits hold that lenity prevails over deference, while others have allowed deference to override lenity in criminal contexts. The issue has grown more salient after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity must be applied before deferring to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations of ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through regulatory interpretation.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference applies even in criminal sentencing contexts, allowing authoritative interpretive commentary to control over the rule of lenity when guidelines text is ambiguous.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) means all three listed criminal-history criteria must be met to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, or whether meeting any single criterion is sufficient. This affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses and creates geographic disparities in criminal sentences.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' commentary (Application Note 1) may expand the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, when the guideline text does not mention them.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor, or whether it impermissibly expands the guideline's plain text. This affects career-offender designations for many federal defendants.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative and binding interpretation of the guideline, and conspiracy and other inchoate offenses qualify as controlled substance offenses for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands the guideline
positions.summary: Holds that the plain text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly adds to the guideline rather than interpreting it, so it is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may be considered as an extraordinary and compelling reason for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether non-retroactive changes in law can contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar consideration of such changes, while others allow them as one factor. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to address this by allowing such claims, but circuits disagree on whether the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as an extraordinary and compelling reason for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be considered
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, as this would circumvent Congress's decision not to make the change retroactive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state law covers substances not listed in the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a prior state drug conviction must involve a substance controlled under federal law to count as a predicate 'controlled substance offense' for sentencing enhancements. Some circuits require a federal-law match, while others hold the Guidelines' text encompasses any substance controlled under state law.

positions.label: Federal-law match not required
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include offenses under state law, even if the specific substance is not federally controlled, because the text contains no federal-law limitation.

positions.label: Federal-law match required
positions.summary: Holds that a prior state conviction must involve a substance also controlled under federal law to qualify as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. De La Rosa

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between the petition date and the conversion date inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, creating a split with other circuits that protect debtors' post-petition equity gains upon good-faith conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), combined with the broad scope of § 541(a)'s definition of estate property, means that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that under the structure and purpose of the Bankruptcy Code, post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal habeas corpus under 28 U.S.C. § 2241 is available to challenge conditions or terms of confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement), or whether prisoners must use civil rights actions (Bivens/§ 1983) for such claims.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is limited to challenges to the fact or duration of confinement, or whether it also extends to challenges to conditions and terms of confinement such as BOP placement decisions and earned time credit calculations under the First Step Act. The Supreme Court has repeatedly noted but not resolved this question since Preiser v. Rodriguez (1973).

positions.label: Habeas available for conditions challenges
positions.summary: Holds that habeas corpus may be used to challenge conditions and terms of confinement, including BOP decisions about placement and credit calculations, when the prisoner alleges statutory violations affecting the manner of custody.

positions.label: Habeas limited to fact/duration of confinement
positions.summary: Holds that habeas corpus is available only to challenge the fact or duration of confinement, and prisoners must use civil rights actions for challenges to conditions or terms of confinement that do not directly affect release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants at supervised release revocation proceedings for the underlying violation, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to the purposes of supervised release.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release at revocation proceedings, placing it at odds with other circuits that hold revocation sentences should serve the purposes of supervised release rather than function as punishment for the violation itself.

positions.label: Punishment permitted at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release at revocation proceedings.

positions.label: Punishment not the purpose of revocation
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation is not meant to punish the defendant for the violation but rather to vindicate the authority of the court and protect the public, and sentences should reflect that limited purpose.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to orders resolving motions for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering the longer civil appeal deadline) or a criminal matter (subject to the shorter criminal appeal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that the Hyde Amendment motion arises from the criminal case and therefore the criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption of state laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier' extends to state tort claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or selection of motor carriers. This affects the availability of tort remedies for injuries caused by trucking accidents arranged by freight brokers.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they relate to the broker's 'service' of arranging transportation, which is within the scope of FAAAA preemption.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent selection of carriers are not preempted because such claims do not sufficiently relate to the broker's prices, routes, or services within the meaning of the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus grounds — such as alleged errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered in compassionate release motions. Some circuits allow such considerations as one factor, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for § 2255 habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claimed legal errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for relitigating the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be brought under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
